exemplar.title: To What Extent Does Temperature Affect the Electrical Resistance and Magnetic Field of Iron and Nickel Wires?

detailedExemplarData.title: To What Extent Does Temperature Affect the Electrical Resistance and Magnetic Field of Iron and Nickel Wires?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Topic is communicated accurately and effectively with a clear, focused purpose throughout.
The aim and research question are explicitly stated, linking temperature changes to measurable properties.
Independent, dependent, and controlled variables are clearly identified, reflecting strong methodological planning.
The method is presented in a logical, sequential format with a complete apparatus list including uncertainties.
weaknesses: Research question phrasing (âTo what extentâ¦â) is somewhat vague and could be more quantitatively specific.
Methodology, while generally appropriate, includes only single measurements per point and omits calibration of the galvanometer.
Some procedural steps and term definitions (e.g. ÎT, Râ) are incomplete or end mid-sentence, reducing clarity.
Background theory does not justify the chosen temperature range or number of trials, limiting methodological justification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place Topic clarity and communication in the top band (5â6) with purposeful focus but minor repetition, Methodology in the middle band (4) due to single readings and incomplete calibration, and Research question in the top band (5â6) albeit phrasing is only partly quantitative. The combined performance fits a score of 5/6, reflecting strong focus and mostly appropriate method with room for tighter specificity and methodological completeness.

strengths: Background theory (Curie temperature, resistivity model) is accurately applied and correctly cited.
Specialized terminology (e.g., temperature coefficient of resistivity, domain misalignment) is used appropriately and consistently.
Ohmâs Law and the model Ï(T)=Ïâ(1+Î±ÎT) are presented correctly, supporting subsequent calculations.
Explanations of lattice vibrations and electron scattering demonstrate clear conceptual understanding.
weaknesses: Occasional unit inconsistencies (switching between mT and ÂµT) and oversimplified quoting of gradients indicate incomplete precision.
Minor imprecision in notation and significantâfigure handling suggests room for deeper mastery.
The application of theory to data sometimes assumes identical gradients without justification, reflecting oversimplification.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates good subject knowledge with mostly relevant application of theory and accurate use of terms, placing both strands in the 3â4 band. Occasional imprecision prevents reaching the top band, so a score of 4/6 appropriately reflects solid but not flawless knowledge and understanding.

strengths: Data analysis moves logically from tables to trends to theoretical interpretation, forming a coherent argument.
Calculations of gradients and percentage uncertainties are correctly applied and clearly presented.
Identification of systematic and random errors and proposed improvements show some critical reflection.
Discussion remains clearly relevant to the research question, linking observations back to theory.
weaknesses: The discussion is largely descriptive and lacks comparison with literature values or quantitative predictions.
Alternative hypotheses are stated but not integrated into the analysis, weakening critical perspective.
Evaluation remains qualitative without quantifying error magnitudes or calibration strategies.
Only single readings per temperature limit depth of statistical evaluation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work shows good research analysis and argument development (bands 4â6) but lacks the sophistication and thorough evaluation needed for the top band. Evaluation work is in the lower part of the 4â6 band. Overall, the studentâs critical thinking merits a mid-band score of 5/12, reflecting solid analysis and argument with moderate critical evaluation.

strengths: Overall structure follows EE conventions with title page, table of contents, and sequential headings.
Data tables and figures are generally clear, with labeled axes and error bars.
High-quality photograph of the experimental setup enhances reproducibility.
Comprehensive data table supports clear analysis.
weaknesses: The abstract is missing and the word limit is exceeded, detracting from professional completeness.
Inconsistent unit notation (mT vs ÂµT) and occasional broken formatting reduce presentation polish.
Duplicated diagrams and nested lists disrupt the narrative flow.
Minor caption errors and low-resolution images prevent maximization of this criterion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is generally clear and appropriately structured, placing both strands in the 3â4 band. However, the absence of an abstract, word-count overrun, formatting inconsistencies, and minor layout issues prevent a top score. A mark of 3/4 accurately reflects these strengths and weaknesses.

strandSelections.reasoning: Specialised terms such as âtemperature coefficient of resistivity,â âCurie point,â âdomain misalignment,â and âuncertainty propagationâ are used largely correctly and consistently.  Occasional slips (switch between mT and ÂµT, âsolenoidâs field measurementâ expressed in volts) show minor imprecision but do not obscure meaning.  Consistency is therefore good rather than flawless, meriting a solid 4 in the 3-4 band.

The narrative moves logically from data tables to plotted trends to theoretical interpretation, forming a coherent argument that temperature increases resistance and decreases magnetic field strength.  However, the discussion is largely descriptive and alternative explanations or literature comparison are limited, preventing it from reaching the sophistication of the top two bands.  A mid-band score of 5 is appropriate.

The experimental plan (constant 2 V supply, water-bath temperature control, five temperature levels, calculation of uncertainties) is generally appropriate and clearly explained; tables list apparatus with uncertainties, and a step-by-step method is supplied.  However, only single measurements per point, a narrow temperature range, lack of calibration of the galvanometer, and limited control of wire cooling constrain completeness.  These weaknesses place the work in the middle band at the upper end (score 4).

Background theory (temperature coefficients, Curie temperature, domain theory) is generally accurate and used to interpret gradients, RÂ² values and uncertainty trends.  Citations are provided and equations are correctly applied.  Some oversimplifications (e.g., identical gradients quoted to three significant figures, unit inconsistencies) reveal incomplete mastery, so the strand aligns with the upper level of the 3-4 band.

The evaluation section identifies several systematic and random errors and proposes reasonable improvements (digital thermometer, rapid transfer, greater number of trials).  The critique remains qualitative; magnitudes of errors, calibration strategies and literature comparison are absent.  Consequently the work shows âsomeâ critical analysis characteristic of the 4-6 band but at the lower end, giving a score of 4.

The student identifies a succinct, discipline-appropriate topic (temperature effects on resistance and magnetism in two ferromagnetic wires) and maintains that focus consistently from the title page through the conclusion.  Clear sign-posting, numbered headings, and repeated linking back to the dual dependent variables in the analysis section show purposeful communication.  Minor lapses (over-long introduction, some repetition) do not materially impede clarity, so overall performance best fits the 5-6 band.

Figures are generally clear with labelled axes, visible error bars and captions; the table layout supports readability.  Nevertheless, duplicated/low-resolution images, misaligned headers and occasional broken mathematical formatting detract from a fully professional presentation.  Hence the work reaches but does not maximise the 3-4 band, justifying a score of 3.

The research question is explicitly stated (âTo what extent does temperature affect the resistance and magnetic field of iron and nickel wires?â) and variables are well defined, allowing a coherent thread in the analysis.  Nonetheless, treating two dependent variables stretches depth, and the phrase âto what extentâ is only partly addressed quantitatively.  These shortcomings reduce precision but the question remains sufficiently focused to remain in the top band, albeit at the lower mark.

Graphs, gradients, and percentage uncertainties are calculated and linked to theory, showing clear relevance to the question. Yet analysis relies on single-point readings, lacks comparison with literature values, and does not calibrate magnetic field measurements, limiting depth. Overall quality is consistent with the 4-6 band, at the mid-level mark of 5.

The essay follows EE conventions (title page, contents, sequential headings, numbered tables/figures).  However, it exceeds the 4 000-word limit, lacks an abstract, and shows inconsistent unit notation and minor caption errors.  These issues prevent full achievement of the top mark in this strand, so the performance sits at 3 within the 3-4 band.

annotations.comment: The Curie temperature is well defined with appropriate citation, demonstrating solid understanding of magnetic phase transitions.
Footnote citations use inconsistent formats. Adopt a standardized citation style (e.g., MLA or APA) and apply it uniformly in the bibliography and footnotes.
The introduction effectively links personal motivation to the topic, demonstrating genuine engagement and a clear rationale for the investigation's scope.

The phrasing âTo What Extentâ¦â in the research question is too vague. Consider specifying the exact quantitative relationship you will measure (e.g., change in resistance per Â°C) to give clearer focus to your investigation.
The word count (4185) exceeds the 4000-word limit. You must reduce content or move material to an appendix to comply with IB formatting requirements.
The topic is clearly and accurately stated, providing a coherent focus on temperature effects in two ferromagnetic materials.
An abstract is missing. Add a concise abstract summarizing the research question, methods, key findings, and conclusion to meet standard EE structure.
The introduction includes extended narrative but lacks succinct connection to the specific methodology. Trim personal background to focus more quickly on your experimental approach.
Background theory lacks justification for the specific temperature range selected in the experiment. Explain why these five temperature points were chosen.
The description of ferromagnetic domains and their alignment is accurate and demonstrates sound subject knowledge of magnetic properties.
The discussion of domain misalignment remains qualitative. Include a quantitative prediction or literature comparison of Curie temperature effects to deepen the analysis.
Safety precautions use inconsistent numbering and formatting. Standardize to a single list style for professional presentation.
The photograph of the experimental setup is clear, well-labeled, and greatly aids reproducibility of your method.
Step 4 of the methodology is incomplete and ends mid-sentence. Provide full, detailed instructions for all procedural steps to avoid ambiguity.
Ohmâs Law is accurately presented, laying a solid theoretical foundation for subsequent calculations of resistance.
The model Ï(T)=Ïâ(1+Î±ÎT) is correctly stated, showing good command of how resistivity varies linearly with temperature.
The itemized list breaks the narrative flow abruptly. Use integrated text with clear subheadings rather than nested HTML lists for readability.
The circuit diagram and its description are duplicated in HTML and alt text. Simplify the presentation to one clear figure with a concise caption to improve layout.
The list defining ÎT and Râ is left incomplete. Clearly define all terms when introducing equations to avoid ambiguity in subsequent calculations.
The independent, dependent, and controlled variables are explicitly identified, reflecting strong methodological planning.
Units for magnetic field switch between mT and ÂµT in later figures. Ensure consistency in unit notation throughout for clarity.
The percentage uncertainty formula is correctly applied and clearly presented, demonstrating strong understanding of error propagation.
The explanation of increased lattice vibrations and their effect on electron scattering is clear and demonstrates appropriate use of theoretical concepts.
Section headings are clear, but the rationale for methodological choices (e.g., number of trials, temperature increments) is not provided. Explain your design decisions.
The aim clearly describes the investigationâs objective, linking temperature changes to measurable physical properties.
The alternative hypothesis is stated but not revisited or tested in the analysis. Integrate this hypothesis into your evaluation to strengthen critical reflection.
Listing âNo Uncertaintyâ for the solenoid is unrealistic. Obtain or estimate the manufacturerâs field-strength tolerance to quantify all uncertainties.
The controlled-variables table omits calibration procedures for the water bath temperature. Include detailed control measures to ensure consistency.
The apparatus list includes all equipment with quantified uncertainties, supporting reproducibility of the experiment.
The method is presented in a logical, sequential format, making it easy to follow and replicate.
Only one reading per temperature was taken. Incorporate repeated trials to calculate statistical averages and reduce random error.
The data table is comprehensive, well-structured and includes all relevant variables, supporting clear analysis.
